coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and copper-doped saffron-based carbon dots: Synthesis, characterization, and cytotoxic effects on human colorectal cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Nemati, Mohadeseh
Hallaj, Tooba
Rezaie, Jafar
Rasmi, Yousef
coredata.dc:description:
Aim Doped carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention in cancer therapy. We aimed to synthesize copper, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Cu, N-CDs) from saffron and investigated their effects on HCT-116 and HT-29 colorectal cancer (CRC) cells.
Main methods CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method and characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. HCT-116 and HT-29 cells were incubated with saffron, N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs for 24 and 48 h for cell viability. Cellular uptake and intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) were evaluated by immunofluorescence microscopy. Oil Red O staining was used to monitor lipid accumulation. Apoptosis was evaluated using acridine orange/propidium iodide (AO/PI) staining and quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (Q-PCR) assay. The expression of miRNA-182 and miRNA-21 was measured by Q-PCR, while the generation of nitric oxide (NO) and lysyl oxidase (LOX) activity was calculated by colorimetric methods.
Key findings CDs were successfully prepared and characterized. Cell viability decreased in the treated cells dose- and time-dependently. HCT-116 and HT-29 cells uptook Cu, N-CDs with a high level of ROS generation. The Oil Red O staining showed lipid accumulation. Concomitant with an up-regulation of apoptotic genes (p < 0.05), AO/PI staining showed increased apoptosis in the treated cells. In comparison to control cells, NO generation, and miRNA-182 and miRNA-21 expression significantly changed in the Cu, N-CDs treated cells (p < 0.05).
Significance The results indicated that Cu, N-CDs could inhibit CRC cells through the induction of ROS generation and apoptosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Saffron
Doped carbon dots
Colorectal Cancer
ROS
Apoptosis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs
Total RNA extraction and cDNA synthesis
The viability of cancer cells was decreased
Intracellular ROS was increased in treated cancer cells
Cu, N-CDs were uptaken by cancer cells
Cu, N-CDs treatment was associated with lipotoxicity
AO/PI staining showed apoptosis induction in cancer cells
Expression of apoptotic genes was increased in Cu, N-CDs treated cancer cells
Expression of miRNA-182 and miRNA-21 was changed in Cu, N-CDs treated cancer cells
Generation of NO was changed in Cu, N-CDs-treated cancer cells
LOX activity of cells was not changed

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Biochemistry and Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported financially by a grant from Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia, Iran. The authors appreciate the personnel of the Department of Biochemistry and Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute for their kind help and support. Data are available from the authors upon reasonable request. A grant number provided by Urmia University of Medical Sciences (IR.UMSU.REC.1400.098) supported the present study. The local ethics committee of Urmia University of Medical Sciences approved all procedures in the present study (Ethical code: IR.UMSU.REC.1400.098). Not applicable.
A grant number provided by Urmia University of Medical Sciences (IR.UMSU.REC.1400.098) supported the present study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs by different methods. TEM analysis for N-CDs (A) and Cu, N-CDs (B). Scale bar: 50 nm. EDX of N-CDs (C). EDX of Cu, N-CDs (D). The FT-IR analysis for N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs (E). UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy of saffron extract (F). The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs (G) and Cu, N-CDs (H). UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy of N-CDs (I) and Cu, N-CDs (J).

MTT assay for HCT-116 and HT-29 cells treated with saffron extract, N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs for 24 and 48 h (A). Representative immunofluorescence images presenting ROS production in saffron extract, N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs treated cells using fluorescence microscopy (B). ×10. Data are prepared as mean ± standard deviation. The means were compared by One-way ANOVA. *P < 0.05 versus control. n = 3.
Uptake of Cu, N-CDs by HCT-116 and HT-29 cells after 2 h of incubation with Cu, N-CDs (A). ×10. Oil Red O staining for HCT-116 and HT-29 cells after treatment with Cu, N-CDs (B). ×20.

AO/PI staining was used to visualize living and dead cells concurrently. Green-stained cells are live, while orange and red cells are apoptotic and necrotic cells, respectively (A). Q-PCR analysis for apoptosis-related genes including Caspase-3, Caspase-8, and Caspase-9 as well as for Akt-1 (B). Data are prepared as mean ± SD. The means were compared by One-way ANOVA. *P < 0.05 and **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 versus control. n = 3.

The relative expression of miRNA-182 and miRNA-21 was measured by Q-PCR (A, A′, B, B′). NO production and LOX activity of cells was measured by colorimetric methods (C, C′, D, D′). Data are prepared as mean ± SD. The means were compared using a t-test. *P < 0.05 and **P < 0.01 versus control. n = 3.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and copper-doped saffron-based carbon dots: Synthesis, characterization, and cytotoxic effects on human colorectal cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Medicine, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Medicine, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Research Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cellular and Molecular Research Medicine Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Research Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Solid Tumor Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Research Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cellular and Molecular Research Medicine Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Solid Tumor Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Research Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Medicine, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doped carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention in cancer therapy. We aimed to synthesize copper, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Cu, N-CDs) from saffron and investigated their effects on HCT-116 and HT-29 colorectal cancer (CRC) cells.

CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method and characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. HCT-116 and HT-29 cells were incubated with saffron, N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs for 24 and 48 h for cell viability. Cellular uptake and intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) were evaluated by immunofluorescence microscopy. Oil Red O staining was used to monitor lipid accumulation. Apoptosis was evaluated using acridine orange/propidium iodide (AO/PI) staining and quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (Q-PCR) assay. The expression of miRNA-182 and miRNA-21 was measured by Q-PCR, while the generation of nitric oxide (NO) and lysyl oxidase (LOX) activity was calculated by colorimetric methods.

CDs were successfully prepared and characterized. Cell viability decreased in the treated cells dose- and time-dependently. HCT-116 and HT-29 cells uptook Cu, N-CDs with a high level of ROS generation. The Oil Red O staining showed lipid accumulation. Concomitant with an up-regulation of apoptotic genes (p < 0.05), AO/PI staining showed increased apoptosis in the treated cells. In comparison to control cells, NO generation, and miRNA-182 and miRNA-21 expression significantly changed in the Cu, N-CDs treated cells (p < 0.05).
The results indicated that Cu, N-CDs could inhibit CRC cells through the induction of ROS generation and apoptosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is known as the second most lethal cancer and the third most prevalent malignant tumor all over the world [1]. In 2020, it had an incidence of about 1.9 million and a mortality of 0.9 million globally [2]. CRC more frequently occurs in younger patients due to risk factors including sedentarism, obesity, smoking, the progressive aging of the population, and bad nutritional habits (high in fats and proteins). In CRC patients, the clinical symptoms depend on the size, location, and presence or absence of metastases. Clinical symptoms includes alteration of chronic bowel habits, abdominal pain, involuntary weight loss, changes in bowel movements, vomiting, nausea, anorexia, abdominal distension, and malaise [3,4]. Metastasis in CRC is a main problem after curative treatment and is the major reason for CRC-related death [5]. The liver is the prevalent organ of distant metastasis in CRC [6,7]. Over the past decade, the progress and use of chemotherapy, immunotherapy, and molecularly targeted therapy have improved patient outcomes, nevertheless, resistance to treatment remains a challenge [8]. Nanoparticles constitute a fast-growing domain of nanotechnology that offers novel potentials for cancer detection and targeted therapy [9]. Therefore, quantum dots (QDs) are critical to the treatment and diagnosis of many cancers [10].

Carbon dots (CDs) are quasispherical and discrete particles with sizes below 10 nm [11] which have demonstrated superior functional characteristics in recent years owing to exceptional physicochemical properties [12]. Green synthesis from natural sources is a desirable method for the preparation of CDs due to properties such as being repeatable, simple, cost-effective, and environment friendly [13,14]. CDs are generally prepared from the carbonization of various chemical carbonic compounds and natural sources such as saffron [15] and Ocimum tenuiflorum [16]. CDs prepared from natural sources show great promise in different fields including, biomedical applications such as bacteria detection [17], radicals scavenging [18], ion sensing [19], and determination of adulterations [16]. For example, CDs prepared from green tea inhibited human breast cancer cells through the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [20]. In addition, Bajpai et al. reported the anticancer activity of nitrogen-phosphorous-doped CDs (N, P-CDs) against melanoma cancer cells and their application in bioimaging [21]. The chemical doping of CDs with non-metal and metal atoms is as an effective method to enhance the physicochemical characteristics of CDs and improve their applications [22]. Copper (Cu) as a metal dopant has been applied to improve the applicability of CDs in various fields. However, Cu may disturb the CDs's electron spin via spin-orbit coupling, reducing electron-hole recombination, and extending the lifetime of charge carriers [23], thereby enhancing the photocatalytic properties of CDs due to their ability to produce ROS [24,25]. On the other hand, Cu (II) complexes showed potent in vitro cytotoxicity and apoptosis against cancer cells, which can be associated with ROS generation [26].

In this study, we prepared N-doped CDs (N-CDs) and Cu, N-doped CDs (Cu, N-CDs) from saffron, as a green precursor, and evaluated the cytotoxic effect of CDs (without any modification of anchoring drugs) on human CRC, HCT-116 and HT-29 cell line.

Pure saffron (Ghaenat Company, Khorasan, Iran), Copper (II) acetate (Merck Millipore), Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) High Glucose (Biosera), Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Biosera), Penicillin-Streptomycin (Pen-Strep, Biosera), Trypsin (Biosera), Phosphate-Buffered Saline (PBS, Sigma-Aldrich), Methyl Thiazolyl diphenyl-Tetrazolium bromide (MTT, Sigma-Aldrich), Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO, Merck Millipore), 2,7-Dichlorofluorescin Diacetate (DCDA, Sigma-Aldrich), Oil Red O (Sigma-Aldrich), Paraformaldehyde (Sigma-Aldrich), Isopropanol (Merk Millipore), Methanol (Merck Millipore), TRIZOL (Sigma-Aldrich), Chloroform (Sigma-Aldrich), Ethanol (Sigma-Aldrich), Acridine Orange/Propidium Iodide (AO/PI, Sigma-Aldrich), Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) reagent (KalaZist), mRNA cDNA synthesis kit (Cat: A101161, Iran), miRNA cDNA synthesis kit (Cat no: BN-0011.17, Iran), mRNAs SYBR Green Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) Master mix (Cat No. YT2551, Iran), miRNAs SYBR Green High ROX Master mix (Cat no: BN-0011.17, Iran), Lysyl Oxidase (LOX) kit (KIAZIST, Iran), Nitric Oxide (NO) kit (KN096, KIAZIST, Iran).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips EM 208, Germany) was used for internal morphology analysis. Elemental analysis of CDs was performed using the energy-dispersive X-ray method (EDX, MIRA3 FEG-SEM, Tescan, USA). The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). A Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Burker, Germany) was employed for functional group analysis. To study the spectral properties of CDs, ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were achieved by a spectrophotometer (Analytic Jena, Spekol 2000, Germany). A microplate reader (BioTek Elx 808, USA) was used to read the absorbance. A fluorescence microscopy (echo LAB microscopy with NFP-1 Fluorescence Microscope Power Supplier Lab, USA) was also used to monitor cellular uptake, ROS generation, AO/PI and Oil Red O staining. MIC Real-Time PCR System (Switzerland) was used for quantitative real-time PCR (Q-PCR) experiments. A thermal cyclers (Bio-Rad, USA) was also used for cDNA synthesis.

To this end, 0.1 g of dried-ground saffron (stigma of Crocus sativus L. flower) was soaked in 10 ml of deionized water with a magnetic stirrer for 15 min at room temperature. Then, the mixture was centrifuged at 9000g for 30 min to eliminate solid residues. It was then filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane to further eliminate trace solid residues and dialyzed in deionized water with the dialysis membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of Cu, N-CDs, at first, 0.1 g of dried-ground saffron and 0.01 g of Cu (II) acetate as the Cu source were mixed in 10 ml of deionized water with a magnetic stirrer for 15 min at room temperature. The obtained mixture was transferred to a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and then heated at 200 °C in an electric furnace for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the reacted solution was centrifuged at 9000g for 30 min to remove large particles. For further purification, the yellow supernatant containing CDs was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and then dialyzed in deionized water through the dialysis membrane. The same synthesis and purification procedures were applied to prepare N-CDs from 0.1 g of saffron mixed in 10 ml of deionized water (without copper II acetate) [15].

HCT-116 and HT-29 cells were obtained from the cell bank of the Pasteur Institute at Iran. The cells were grown in DMEM high glucose medium supplemented with 10 % FBS and, 1 % unit/ml pen-strep and kept in a humidified atmosphere (95 %) with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. The cells in the 3rd-6th passages were used for all the experiments with three biological repeats.

MTT assay was performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity effects of saffron, N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs. In this method, the mitochondrial enzyme activity in living cells was measured, which is based on the conversion of the soluble yellow MTT solution to an insoluble purple formazan product. First, 5 × 103 HCT-116 and HT-29 cells were seeded into each well of a 96-well culture plate for 24 h. Subsequently, HCT-116 and HT-29 were treated with different doses of saffron (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 mg), N-CDs (0.156, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, and 1.25 mg) and, Cu, N-CDs (0.156, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, and 1.25 mg) solutions and incubated for 24 and 48 h. Next, 20 μl of the freshly prepared MTT solution (5 mg/ml) was added and incubated for 4 h to permit purple-colored formazan formation. The supernatant was removed and the DMSO solution was added to dissolve formazan. The adsorption was read at 570 nm using a microplate reader. The cells cultured with DMEM were used as controls. The following equation was used to calculate cell survival [27]:

To investigate Cu, N-CDs uptake by cancer cells, HCT-116 and HT-29 cells (3 × 104 per well) were seeded in a plate and kept overnight. After treatment with Cu, N-CDs for 2 h, the cell conditioned medium was discarded, and the cells were twice washed with PBS. Then, images were captured using the fluorescence microscope.

To explore the generation of intracellular ROS, we used a fluorometric intracellular ROS kit with fluorescence microscopy. In brief, HCT-116 and HT-29 cells (5 × 104 cells per well) were incubated in a 12-well plate for 24 h. After the treatment protocol, the cells were incubated with DCDA for 15 min at room temperature. Then, they were washed with PBS and imaged by fluorescence microscopy.

We performed the Oil Red O staining to investigate lipotoxicity. Briefly, 2 × 104 (per well) were cultured in a 24-well plate overnight. After treatment, HCT-116 and HT-29 cells were washed and kept in methanol for 15 min and then fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde solution and kept with 0.1 % Oil Red O staining solution for 20 min. After triple washing with PBS, images were taken by light microscopy.

To determine apoptosis induction in cells, HCT-116 and HT-29 cells (5 × 104 cells per well) were incubated in an 8-well slide-chamber plate for 24 h. After the treatment protocol, the supernatant was discarded, and the cells were washed with PBS. Then, under dark conditions, 500 μl AO/PI solution was diluted with PBS (500 μl), and 50 μl of the AO/PI solution was added to each well. After 5 min of incubation, the plate was viewed under fluorescence microscopy using a 20× objective lens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total RNA extraction and cDNA synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Total RNA was extracted from HCT-116 and HT-29 cells (1 × 106 cells) using the conventional TRIZOl protocol. In brief, 1 ml of the TRIZOl solution was added to the cells and kept for 5 min on an ice pad. After adding 200 μl of chloroform, the mixtures were centrifuged at 12,000g for 15 min at 4 °C. Then, the upper phase was collected and mixed with cold isopropanol and kept for 20 min at −20 °C. The mixture was centrifuged at 12,000g for 15 min at 4 °C for pellet RNA. Next, the RNA pellet was twice washed with ethanol solution (75 %) at 7500g for 5 min at 4 °C. Finally, the RNA pellet was dissolved in diethyl pyrocarbonate (DEPC) water and kept at −80 °C for downstream experiments. To produce the reverse transcriptase (RT)-First Strand cDNA, we used a cDNA synthesis kit for mRNA and miRNA experiments. According to the kit's instructions, for mRNA, 1 μg of the total RNA was mixed with oligo (dT) 18 primer or random hexamer primer and incubated for 5 min at 70 °C. Next, the cDNA synthesis components mix was added to the mixture and incubated for 60 min at 42 °C. To terminate the reaction, the samples were heated at 70 °C for 5 min. For miRNA, 2 μg of total RNA was mixed with BON-RT adaptor, then reagent RT (mixture of enzyme, buffer, and deoxynucleotide triphosphate (dNTP)) was added and reactions were completed using a thermal cyclers with a temperature/duration program. The reverse transcription reaction products were stored at −80 °C until use.

To evaluate the expression levels of genes involved in apoptosis, including caspase-9, caspase-8, and caspase-3, as well as miRNA-182 and miRNA-21, they were evaluated using Q-PCR assay. According to the manufacturer's instructions, proper cDNA samples were mixed with the SYBR Green PCR Master mix for mRNAs and SYBR Green High ROX Master mix for miRNAs in a MIC Real-Time PCR System. For the mRNA experiment, cycles were set as follows: 95 °C for 5 min (1 cycle), 95 °C for 10 s, (54 °C for Akt-1, 55.5 °C for caspase-3, and 60 °C for caspase-8 and caspase-9) for 30 s, 72 °C for 20 s (40 cycles); and for the miRNAs experiment: 95 °C for 2 min (1 cycle), 95 °C for 5 s, 60 °C for 30 s (40 cycles). The expression levels of the genes were calculated by the comparative 2 (−ΔΔCT) method. The relative expression of target genes was controlled against the housekeeping genes. A list of primers is presented in Table 1 .

To investigate LOX enzyme activity in the cancer cells, we used a simple colorimetric method using a commercial kit. Briefly, HCT-116 and HT-29 cells (1 × 106 cells) were lysed by LOX Lysis Buffer supplemented with a protease inhibitor cocktail; then, a freeze-melt-refreeze cycle was performed. After centrifuging at 12,000g for 15 min at 4 °C, the supernatant was collected, and the protein concentration was measured using a BSA reagent. Next, 50 μl of the samples were mixed with 5 μl of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), 250 μl of LOX Substrate Buffer, and 1 μl of Lox Probe and transferred into wells of a 96-well plate. Absorbance was measured at times 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 min by a microplate reader at a 570-nm wavelength. LOX activity was defined as Δ570 treatment/Δ570 control.

To measure NO generation, the enzymatic conversion of nitrate into nitrite was measured by Griess reaction and a commercial kit. In brief, 1 × 106 cells were lysed by NO buffer containing a protease inhibitor cocktail. The samples were frozen and unfroze in triplicate. The samples were centrifuged at 12,000g for 15 min at 4 °C and the supernatant was saved. Then, deproteinizer 1 and 2 were added and centrifuged at 6000g for 10 min. Equal volumes of the supernatant were mixed with the Griess reagent and incubated for 12 h at room temperature. The optical absorbance was detected using a microplate reader at a 545-nm wavelength. The standard curve was prepared by serial dilutions of nitrate.

The statistical significance between groups was calculated by multivariate analysis of valiance (ANOVA) with Tukey's post-hoc test. In addition, a t-test was performed to compare the two groups. The data were analyzed in GraphPad Prism version 9.0.1 (USA). All of the measurements were performed at least in triplicate and results were reported as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Differences at P < 0.05 were considered significant.

The TEM images showed that the spherical and monodispersed CDs were successfully synthesized. The easy and environment-friendly treatment resulted in the as-synthesized CDs possessing a uniform particle size with a mean diameter of 8 ± 2 nm for N-CDs (Fig. 1A) and 12 ± 2 nm for Cu, N-CDs (Fig. 1B). As shown in Fig. 1 (C, D, E) FT-IR and EDX analyses were performed to study the structure and surface functional groups of the as-prepared CDs. Fig. 1 indicates the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs (Fig. 1E). Strong peaks around 3400 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1 were identified as the stretching vibrations of the NH or OH and CH, respectively. Furthermore, the bands appearing at 1400 cm−1, 1615 cm−1, and 1670 cm−1 were ascribed to -COO and CN, CC, CO functional groups. In comparison to N-CDs, in the FT-IR spectrum of Cu, N-CDs new peaks appeared at around 900–1020 cm−1 which were identified as an N-Cu-N stretching vibration [25]. Additionally, the characteristic peaks of N-CDs at 1710 cm−1, 1225 cm−1, and 1365 cm−1, related to CO, CO and CN, were found to decrease in Cu, N-CDs [28]. These results confirm the formation of Cu coordination complexes in Cu, N-CDs. Furthermore, examining the elemental composition of Cu, N-CDs using EDX indicated the presence of 53 % C, 6 % N, 39 % O, and 2 % Cu in the structure of Cu, N-CDs (Fig. 1D) (The EDX analysis of N-CDs is in Fig. 1C).

To explore the optical properties of as-prepared CDs, the fluorescence and UV–Vis spectra of N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 1G and H, both CDs showed an excitation wavelength-dependent emission behavior. The excitation wavelength was altered from 360 to 480-nm, with increments of 20-nm. The maximum emission peaks of both CDs were the same and located at 510 nm with a 460-nm excitation wavelength. Note that the saffron extract showed no fluorescence properties. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of prepared N-CDs (Fig. 1I) and Cu, N-CDs (Fig. 1J) were different from the saffron extract (Fig. 1F). Both CDs indicated the general UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs including a strong peak in the UV area with a tail spreading into the visible range. The absorption band around 280-nm was attributed to the π-π* of CC and the shoulder appearing at the range of 320–380-nm was ascribed to n–π* transitions of CO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The viability of cancer cells was decreased
ce:section.ce:para: The toxicity of saffron, N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs was investigated via an MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 2A, we found that the viability of the cells decreased with an increase in the concentrations of saffron, N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs. The IC50 values were 1.78 mg/ml and 1.21 mg/ml for the saffron extract, 0.52 mg/ml and 0.37 mg/ml for N-CDs, and 0.42 mg/ml and 0.28 mg/ml for Cu, N-CDs throughout 24 and 48 h of incubation of HCT-116, respectively. The IC50 values were 1.53 mg/ml and 1.26 mg/ml for the saffron extract, 0.54 mg/ml and 0.33 mg/ml for N-CDs, and 0.38 mg/ml and 0.26 mg/ml for Cu, N-CDs throughout 24 and 48 h of incubation of HT-29, respectively. These results suggested that saffron, N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs induced considerable toxicity toward HCT-116 and HT-29 cells as compared to the control cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular ROS was increased in treated cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the cellular cytotoxicity caused by saffron, N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs, we evaluated the generation of ROS in HCT-116 and HT-29 cells. The oxidation of DCDA which is a non-fluorescent substrate to a green fluorescent product is a technique used for the detection of intracellular ROS, especially hydrogen peroxide. The cell membrane is permeable to esterified forms of DCDA; therefore, they can pass through cells without restrictions and become trapped intracellularly with the deacetylation function of intracellular esterases [29]. We found ROS generation was increased in cells treated with Cu, N-CDs after 24 h (Fig. 2B). The ROS production was more prominent in the Cu, N-CDs treated cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu, N-CDs were uptaken by cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake assay demonstrated that the Cu, N-CDs were abundantly uptaken by HCT-116 and HT-29 cells (Fig. 3A). This showed that Cu, N-CDs could pass through the cancer cells' plasma membrane and accumulate in the cytoplasm, suggesting the possible anti-cancer application of Cu, N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu, N-CDs treatment was associated with lipotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: To further study cellular cytotoxicity, by Oil Red O staining, we found numerous Oil Red O-positive cancer cells co-cultured with Cu, N-CDs, indicating the accumulation of lipids in the cytoplasm and cellular lipotoxicity [30] (Fig. 3B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: AO/PI staining showed apoptosis induction in cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The AO/PI double staining assay was performed to microscopically study the cell death of HCT-116 and HT-29 cells. In this context, viable cancer cells are stained green color, while green-stained cancer cells displaying some apoptotic properties such as chromatin condensation and membrane blebbing are early apoptotic cells. In addition, orange-stained cells are late apoptotic cells, while red-stained ones are necrotic cells [31]. Fig. 4A displays the stained cancer cells treated with saffron, N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs undergoing apoptosis. The presence of reddish-orange color, due to the binding of AO to the denatured DNA, was detected in the treated cells. Furthermore, the cells exposed to Cu, N-CDs showed more severe apoptosis, including late apoptosis, membrane blebbing, apoptotic bodies, and necrosis (Fig. 4A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression of apoptotic genes was increased in Cu, N-CDs treated cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative measurement of apoptosis was carried out by applying the Q-PCR assay. As compared control cells, the expression levels of caspase-3 were elevated in the cells exposed to saffron (1.3 ± 0.05-fold change, p < 0.001 and 1.3 ± 0.12-fold change), N-CDs (2.4 ± 0.37-fold change and 2.4 ± 0.02-fold change, p < 0.001), and Cu, N-CDs (2.5 ± 0.35-fold change and 3.5 ± 0.26-fold change, p < 0.001) in HCT-116 and HT-29 cell lines respectively (Fig. 4B). The mRNA levels of caspase-8 were significantly up-regulated in N-CDs (3.7 ± 0.30-fold change, p < 0.001) and Cu, N-CDs (4 ± 0.79-fold change, p < 0.001) compared to saffron and control groups in HCT-116 cell line. In the case of HT-29 cells, as compared to control cells, the expression of caspase-8 decreased in saffron treated cells (p < 0.001) but increased in N-CDs-treated cells (p < 0.05). Moreover, the expression of caspase-8 in N-CDs increased compared to saffron and Cu, N-CDs-treated cells (p < 0.05).

The same results were observed in the expression of caspase-9 (Fig. 4B). The expression of caspase-9 increased in the N-CDs group (4.4 ± 0.18-fold change, p < 0.01) and Cu, N-CDs group (7.02 ± 1.74-fold change, p < 0.001) compared to saffron and control groups in the HCT-116 cell line. Compared to the control group, in the HT-29 cell line, the expression of caspase-9 was decreased in saffron-treated cells (0.29 ± 0.01-fold change, p < 0.01) but, increased in Cu, N-CDs-treated cells (2.49 ± 0.18-fold change, p < 0.001) compared to the control (Fig. 4B). In addition, we found that the expression of caspase-9 in Cu, N-CDs-treated cells was up-regulated compared with saffron and N-CDs-treated cells (p < 0.001).

We also found that the caspase-9/caspase-8 ratio was increased in HCT-116 cells treated with Cu, N-CDs (1.74 ± 0.08-fold change) compared with the control (p < 0.01) and other groups (p < 0.05 and p < 0.01) (Fig. 4B). This ratio increased in Cu, N-CDs-treated HT-29 cells compared to all groups (2.87 ± 0.14 fold change, p < 0.001).

The results also showed a significant increase in the mRNA levels of Akt-1 in the cells exposed to N-CDs (4.7 ± 0.63-fold change, p < 0.001) and Cu, N-CDs (3.4 ± 0.12-fold change, p < 0.001) in the HCT-116 cell line compared to saffron-treated cells and the control (Fig. 4B). In another cell line, HT-29 cells, compared to control and saffron groups, the expression of Akt-1 increased in N-CDs (1.50 ± 0.70-fold change, p < 0.05) and Cu, N-CDs (1.14 ± 0.35-fold change, p < 0.05). The expression levels of Akt-1 were not significantly altered in saffron-treated cells (0.98 ± 0.10-fold change and 0.81 ± 0.22-fold change in both HCT-116 and HT-29 cell lines respectively) (Fig. 4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression of miRNA-182 and miRNA-21 was changed in Cu, N-CDs treated cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Expression levels of miRNA-182 were down-regulated in Cu, N-CDs compared to the control (0.738 ± 0.18-fold change, p < 0.05).The expression levels of the miRNA-21 increased in Cu, N-CDs compared to the control (1.6 ± 0.27-fold change, p < 0.05, Fig. 5A, B) in the HCT-116 cell line, but expression levels of miRNA-182 (0.53 ± 0.25-fold change, p < 0.05), miRNA-21 (0.29 ± 0.14-fold change, p < 0.05) were down-regulated in Cu, N-CDs compared to the control in the HT-29 cell line (Fig. 5A′, B′).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of NO was changed in Cu, N-CDs-treated cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: We performed a Griess assay to evaluate the level of NO generation in cancer cells. The findings revealed that NO concentration significantly decreased in treated HCT-116 cells compared to control cells (66.91 ± 10.15 against 209. 7 ± 10.11, p < 0.01,) (Fig. 5C), but there was a high level of the NO concentration in Cu, N-CDs treated HT-29 cells compared to the control (234.9 ± 11.19 against 161.3 ± 9.92, p < 0.05, Fig. 5C′).

ce:section.ce:section-title: LOX activity of cells was not changed
ce:section.ce:para: We analyzed LOX activity in treated cells by colorimetric assay and found a non-significant increase in the activity of LOX in Cu, N-CDs-treated cancer cells (1.034 ± 0.036 and 1.074 ± 0.028, in HCT-116 and HT-29 cells, respectively, p > 0.05,) (Fig. 5D, D′).

We synthesized stable and water-soluble CDs from saffron and prepared Cu, N-CDs nanoparticles to target the CRC cell nucleus to kill the tumor cells through the apoptosis pathway. We selected saffron for CDs synthesis due to its abundance, antitumor properties, and being a rich elemental source of C, O, and N [32–35]. Subsequently, Cu, N-CDs were synthesized for the first time by a simple one-step hydrothermal process. The formation of N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs were characterized by different methods. TEM analysis confirmed the formation of monodispersed N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs with spherical shapes. The saffron extract and saffron-based CDs possessed good aqueous solubility because of many hydrophilic groups on the surface, as shown by different spectroscopic methods. Besides, the FT-IR and EDX results demonstrated the successful synthesis and doping process of Cu, N-CDs.

The second objective was to investigate the anticancer effects of CDs on human CRC cells, HCT-116 and HT-29, by different experiments. The viability of the cells treated with saffron, N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs decreased in a dose and time-dependent manner. The IC50 value of Cu, N-CDs was 0.42 mg/ml and 0.38 mg/ml for 24 h of treatment in HCT-116 and HT-29 cells, respectively. Based on these findings, we concluded that Cu, N-CDs have more cytotoxic effects on cancer cells (Fig. 2A). It was previously reported that Cu-CDs are more effective than CDs in inhibiting the growth of multi-cellular spheroids as a three-dimensional tissue model (3D MCs) [24]. Hence, the Cu-CDs may soon become a promising tool for cancer therapy [24]. These results provide additional support for the effective anticancer properties of CDs in vitro [36]. In the next step, we confirmed that CDs can be internalized by cancer cells, a feature that would be promising for cancer imaging and drug delivery systems [37]. We also hypothesized whether the accumulation CDs inside the cytoplasm may cause ROS generation. As shown by Fig. 2B, we observed intracellular ROS generation in treated cancer cells, indicating cellular cytotoxicity and oxidative stress [38]. It has been reported that CDs prepared from ginger can suppress human hepatocellular carcinoma cells and increase ROS generation in the cancer cells [39]. However, we found that cells treated with Cu, N-CDs showed a high level of intracellular ROS in both cell lines, probably due to delivering Cu into cells [26]. It is known that Cu can increase ROS generation in the Fenton-like reaction, which can explain the high level of ROS generation in cancer cells treated with Cu, N-CDs. Chudal et al. have also reported that copper cysteamine (Cu-Cy) nanoparticles (NPs) can participate in a Fenton-like activity under acidic conditions to generate higher levels of ROS in cancer cells compared to normal cells and show significantly more destruction to cancerous cells selectively without the need for exogenous additives [40].

Additional evidence for the high cellular cytotoxicity of Cu, N-CDs came from the AO/PI assay where we observed that Cu, N-CDs treatment markedly induced apoptosis in cells. Our results are in line with previous results demonstrating that ROS generation can trigger apoptosis in cells [41,42]. ROS generation can damage different organelles and drive cellular signaling [43]. A further illustration of the potential underlying cytotoxic effects of Cu, N-CDs might be taken from the Q-PCR analysis. To achieve further molecular insight into the apoptosis pathway, we analyzed the expression of caspase-3, caspase-8, and caspase-9 in treated cells. Caspases are a family of protease enzymes playing an important function in programmed cell death [44]. The expression of caspase-3 was significantly up-regulated in the treated cells, suggesting apoptosis induction [45]. The caspase-9/caspase-8 ratio increased in N-CDs, and Cu, N-CDs in the HCT-116 cell line and in Cu, N-CDs in the HT-29 cell line, which may correlate with the activation of apoptosis through the interstice pathway [45]. The evidence we found may point to the mitochondria damage caused by Cu and ROS accumulation, triggering apoptosis, although we did not assay mitochondria damage [46,47]. It was concluded that Cu (II) complexes (2 and 5) show potent in vitro cytotoxicity and apoptosis against MCF-7 cells, which can be associated with ROS generation [26]. For more profound cytotoxic effects, we treated the cells with IC50 of Cu, N-CDs for downstream experiments. In addition, there is evidence to support the hypothesis that the accumulation of ROS can impair lipid metabolism and cause lipotoxicity in cells, which confirms our Oil Red O staining results [48]; we found that treated cells were abundantly positive for Oil Red O staining. Lipotoxicity is known to induce cell death [49].

Also, we found that the expression of Akt-1 was up-regulated in Cu, N-CDs-treated cells. Akt plays important functions in different cellular processes including glucose metabolism, cell migration, cell death, proliferation, and transcription [50]. The abnormal expression or activation of Akt has been reported in many cancers. Although Akt is recognized as a survival kinase, the inhibition of Akt does not always induce extensive apoptosis [51]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of its kind, and further scrutiny is required as Akt controls different biological processes and we only measured its mRNA levels. NO modulates various cancer-related processes comprising apoptosis, invasion, angiogenesis, cell cycle, and metastasis [52]. Increased NO generation in a cell may contribute to tumor angiogenesis [53]. However, some researchers demonstrated that increased NO can damage cancer cells [52]. There is great controversy and confusion in understanding the exact role of NO in cancer progression. The low-level NO generated from HCT-116-treated cells may significantly contribute to decreased angiogenesis [52], and high-level NO produced by HT-29-treated cells may damage cancer cells. These divergent results may be related to the type of cell line, and more investigations are warranted.

We also studied the important miRNAs involved in the biological process under oxidative stress including miRNA-182 and miRNA-21. We found an increase in miRNA-21 and a decrease in miRNA-182 in HCT-116 treated cells and a decline in miRNA-21 and miRNA-182 in HT-29 treated cells. The miRNA-182 overexpressed in CRC [54], participated in CRC progression and could be used as a potential prognostic biomarker and therapeutic target in CRC management [55]. Previous studies have shown that miRNA-182 inhibits apoptosis in cancer cells [56]. It is well established that miRNA-21 targets several important components in a wide range of tumors and acts on different cellular processes ranging from tumor cell stemness to apoptosis. The interplay between ROS and miRNA-21 reveals that increased ROS can contribute to the up-regulation of miRNA-21 [57,58]. Based on these data, it can be assumed that the expression pattern of these miRNAs was attributed to increased ROS and apoptosis rate; nevertheless, the detailed mechanisms remain to be elucidated. Furthermore, studies have shown that the inhibition of miRNA-21 expression led to hypersensitivity of HT-29 cells to 5-FU, while further overexpression of miRNA-21 promoted cell proliferation [59]. We also observed that miRNA-21 was reduced in HT-29 treated cells with Cu-N-CDs. The miRNA-21 expression also showed contradictory results in the two cell lines, which may depend on the type of cell lines.

In summary, we successfully synthesized N-CDs and Cu, N-CDs from saffron via a simple one-step hydrothermal process. Through different experiments including MTT, ROS, apoptosis, Oil Red O staining, miRNAs, and NO examination, we demonstrated that CDs exert cytotoxic effects on CRC cells and can inhibit tumorigenesis. To the best of our knowledge, it seems that Cu, N-CDs have more cytotoxicity against saffron and N-CDs. At this stage of understanding, one can hypothesize that the progress in HCT-116 and HT-29 cell death was associated with the increased ROS and oxidative stress; however, further scrutiny may elucidate the possible mechanism in this situation.

The evidence from this study indicated that N-CDs were successfully prepared from saffron and doped with Cu. They efficiently inhibited cancer cells by inducing ROS and intrinsic apoptosis pathways. A more important finding was that Cu, N-CDs were successfully accumulated inside the cells synchronized with an increase in ROS and cell cytotoxicity, which might contribute to cell death. The current findings are significant in at least two respects: (i) These results show that CDs successfully delivered Cu to cancer cells with increased apoptosis; (ii) These results may help advance current cancer treatments and imaging methods. Therefore, our green-synthesized CDs could be useful in the treatment of CRC, while in vivo studies are required to further validate the practical application of CDs.
Mohadeseh Nemati: Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation, Investigation, Visualization.
Tooba Hallaj: Investigation, Methodology, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.
Jafar Rezaie: Software, Investigation, Methodology, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.
Yousef Rasmi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declared that there is no financial or non-financial interests that are directly or indirectly related to this work.

ce:section.ce:para: This study was supported financially by a grant from Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia, Iran. The authors appreciate the personnel of the Department of Biochemistry and Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute for their kind help and support.
Data are available from the authors upon reasonable request.
A grant number provided by Urmia University of Medical Sciences (IR.UMSU.REC.1400.098) supported the present study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethical approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: The local ethics committee of Urmia University of Medical Sciences approved all procedures in the present study (Ethical code: IR.UMSU.REC.1400.098).
Not applicable.
